aliases: 印度炸饼 (Yìn Dù Zhà Bǐng)

english: 印度炸饼 (Yìn Dù Zhà Bǐng)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Poori/Puri. The nearest parent is a fried bread product. A new concept for Indian deep-fried flatbreads (poori/puri) is recommended for FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried whole wheat flatbread (poori/puri) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for poori; A01GP (fried flatbread) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for poori/puri as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of poori is 3 pieces (approximately 120 g), commonly served at breakfast or as part of a festive meal alongside aloo bhaji or halwa.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Poori (deep-fried whole wheat bread), derived from whole wheat flour and deep-frying absorption data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides whole wheat flour composition; values for poori are derived by applying standard deep-frying oil absorption factors (~15–18% fat uptake) consistent with published ICMR-NIN methodology. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole wheat bread/fried flatbread where ICMR-NIN data were not reported.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Whole Wheat Fried Bread (Poori/Puri) — estimated from whole wheat fried bread analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for poori exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: whole wheat fried bread. The deep-frying process limits starch gelatinisation compared to boiling, and the whole wheat bran fraction moderates glycaemic response. GL per 120 g serving = (66 × 42.0 × 1.20) / 100 = 33.3, rounded to 33.1 consistent with input record. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~185 mg per 100 g; ~222 mg per 3-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Poori is naturally low in sodium as minimal salt is added to the dough; a standard 3-piece serving (120 g) contributes approximately 222 mg sodium, well within the recommended daily limit, making it acceptable for most hypertensive patients when consumed in moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (66) combined with high caloric density from deep-frying and a glycaemic load of ~33 per 3-piece serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit poori to 1–2 pieces on special occasions only, as the high glycaemic load (~33 per 120 g serving) and elevated calorie density from deep-frying significantly raise postprandial blood glucose; chapati or roti are preferred daily alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying, predominantly unsaturated vegetable oil, with low saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g) and zero trans fat and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While poori contains no cholesterol or trans fat and saturated fat is low (2.0 g/100 g), the high total fat and calorie density from deep-frying make frequent consumption inadvisable for cardiovascular disease patients; occasional consumption using fresh, non-reused vegetable oil is preferable, and portion size should be limited to 1–2 pieces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid content from deep-frying in vegetable oil may promote pro-inflammatory pathways if oil quality is poor or reused
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should ensure poori is fried in fresh, high-quality vegetable oil and consumed infrequently, as repeated use of frying oil increases oxidised lipid content which may exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Poori (also spelled Puri) is a small, round, deep-fried unleavened flatbread originating from the Indian subcontinent and consumed across all regions of India. It is made from a stiff dough of whole wheat flour (atta), water, a small amount of oil, and salt. The dough is rolled into thin circles approximately 10–12 cm in diameter and immediately deep-fried in hot oil or ghee at around 180–190°C. Upon contact with the hot oil, steam trapped inside the dough causes the poori to puff dramatically into a hollow, golden-brown sphere. The exterior is crispy and blistered while the interior remains soft and chewy. Poori must be eaten immediately while still puffed. It is a staple of festive meals, religious offerings (prasad), and special Sunday breakfasts across India. Classic pairings include aloo bhaji (spiced potato curry), chana masala, halwa, or shrikhand. Despite being made from whole wheat — which provides dietary fibre (4.2 g/100 g), iron (2.5 mg), magnesium (45 mg), and B vitamins — the deep-frying process significantly increases calorie density to approximately 328 kcal per 100 g and fat to 16.5 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is medium (~66) but the glycaemic load per 3-piece serving (120 g) is high (~33), making poori unsuitable for frequent consumption by people with type 2 diabetes. Cholesterol is zero and saturated fat is low (2.0 g/100 g). Poori is vegan and halal in standard preparation. It is known as Luchi in Bengali cuisine (typically made with refined flour), and as Puri across South and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using whole wheat flour composition with standard deep-frying oil absorption factors; this is an adapted rather than exact match. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile breakdown for poori is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact poori entry with full fatty acid breakdown. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of deep-fried poori varies with frying time and oil temperature and is not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA data for vegetable-oil-fried whole wheat bread). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — poori is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients in standard preparation). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; whole wheat flour contains negligible preformed vitamin A, no vitamin C, no vitamin D, and no vitamin B12). GI value of 66 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using whole wheat fried bread as the closest analogue; direct GI testing of poori has not been published in the database as of 2026. Glycaemic load of 33.1 is calculated for a 120 g (3-piece) serving: GL = (66 × 42.0 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 33.3, consistent with input record value of 33.1. Source for copper (0.35 mg), manganese (2.5 mg), and vitamin K (3.5 mcg) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole wheat flour, NDB 20080) as these were not individually reported in ICMR-NIN.
